basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Takwīr — “The Folding Up” — opens with a string of twelve conditions, all in when, raising the total picture of the yawm al-qiyāma: sun folded up, stars dimmed, mountains set in motion, pregnant she-camels left untended, wild beasts gathered, seas brought to a boil, souls paired, the buried infant questioned about the crime of her murder, pages spread, heaven flayed, Furnace stoked, Garden brought near. At the end of this cascade, an answer: every soul shall know what it has prepared. But Qushayrī then opens an astonishing reading: these conditions do not describe only the cosmic Resurrection of tomorrow — they also describe the inner resurrection lived by the Sufis today. “When these states seize them and these meanings manifest to their hearts, these things occur. Their suns sometimes know an eclipse, when they are sent back [to qabḍ after basṭ]; the stars of their sciences dim through the grip of passion upon the disciples. And at that moment, every soul knows what it has prepared.” Then come the oaths by the planets that fade, by the night that falls, by the dawn that breathes its breath — to proclaim that this Word is that of a noble messenger (Gabriel), and that your companion (Muḥammad) has seen him at the clear horizon. And the sūra closes on the great question: fa-ayna tadhhabūn — where then are you going?

versets.trad: When the sun is folded up,
versets.commentaire: kuwwirat: has been rolled up, folded upon itself like a turban. Qushayrī, in a word: “its light has vanished.” Striking image: the sun does not go out — it folds itself, as one folds a luminous cloth.

mots.def: kawwara: to roll up; in the passive feminine perfect.
mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

versets.trad: when the stars are dimmed,
versets.commentaire: inkadarat: Qushayrī: “they scattered and fell upon the earth.” The root k-d-r names trouble, opacity — stars whose clarity is dimmed.

mots.def: inkadara: to grow troubled, dim, to fall.

versets.trad: when the mountains are set in motion,
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī: “their peaks were removed.” The mountains — image of the immutable itself — set themselves walking. If they move, what remains firm?

mots.def: sayyara: to set walking; passive feminine perfect.
mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

versets.trad: when the pregnant she-camels are left untended,
versets.commentaire: al-ʿishār (العِشَار): Qushayrī: “these are the pregnant she-camels at ten months of gestation.” For the Arabs of the desert, these she-camels were the most precious wealth. ʿuṭṭilat: left fallow, without care. Qushayrī: “Left untended that day by reason of the intensity of the dread.” The image speaks of the abandonment of all wealth before the absolute urgency.

mots.def: ʿishār: she-camels at ten months of gestation.
ʿaṭṭala in the passive; to leave fallow.
mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

versets.trad: when the wild beasts are gathered,
versets.commentaire: al-wuḥūsh: the wild beasts. Qushayrī: “brought back to life and gathered at the Resurrection for justice to be done among them: justice will be rendered to the hornless [beast] against the horned one (according to Ibn ʿAbbās). And this is by way of example, for there is no moral accountability upon the beasts.” On the Day, even animal justice is rendered: no wrong is overlooked.

mots.def: ḥashara in the passive; to gather.
mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

versets.trad: when the seas are brought to a boil,
versets.commentaire: sujjirat: Qushayrī: “they were set ablaze — from sajartu al-tannūr, « I heated the oven ».” The image is terrible: oceans become furnaces.

mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

versets.trad: when the souls are paired,
versets.commentaire: zuwwijat: paired with their like. Qushayrī: “paired with their mates. The ḥadīth says: « Each man is joined to the group whose deeds resembled his own. »” Each finds himself with his spiritual peers — that, already, will be part of the sentence.

mots.def: zawwaja in the passive; to pair.
mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

versets.trad: when the girl buried alive is asked
versets.commentaire: al-mawʾūda (المَوْءُودَة): the girl buried alive — a practice known in pre-Islamic Arabia, where one sometimes buried daughters at birth out of fear of shame or poverty. The Quran absolutely abolishes the practice, and here makes the victim the subject of the trial, not its object: it is she who is questioned. The sūra thus reverses the executioner/victim relationship.

mots.def: mawʾūda: passive participle of waʾada; buried alive.
saʾala in the passive feminine perfect.
mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

versets.trad: for what crime she was killed,
versets.commentaire: The question is addressed to the girl, not to the murderer: for what crime were you killed? In Arabic grammar, it is even more striking: it is she who is questioned, as though an answer were awaited from her — knowing that she has none. The question, in its very structure, is the condemnation of the murderer.

mots.def: qatala in the passive feminine perfect.
mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

versets.trad: when the [sacred] pages are spread,
versets.commentaire: nushirat — Qushayrī, in one word: “opened, spread.” The registers of deeds, hitherto closed, are unrolled. Each soul reads its story in them.

mots.def: nashara in the passive; to spread.
mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

versets.trad: when the heaven is stripped of its covering,
versets.commentaire: kushiṭat: Qushayrī: “torn off and folded.” The verb names flaying — the heaven is stripped of its skin as a beast of its fur.

mots.def: kashaṭa in the passive; to flay.
mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

versets.trad: when the Furnace is stoked,
versets.commentaire: suʿʿirat: kindled, stoked to its utmost.

mots.def: saʿʿara in the passive; to stoke.
mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

versets.trad: when the Garden is brought near,
versets.commentaire: uzlifat: Qushayrī: “brought near to the pious.” The Garden does not wait — it comes forward to meet those who have prepared it.

mots.def: azlafa in the passive; to bring near.
mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

versets.trad: every soul shall know what it has prepared.
versets.commentaire: The answer-verse of the sūra, that closes the twelve conditions. But Qushayrī then opens a reading that changes everything: “In the inner qiyāma of this group [the Sufis] at the moment when these states seize them and these meanings manifest to their hearts, these things occur. Among their various states: their suns sometimes know an eclipse, when they are sent back [to qabḍ after basṭ]; the stars of their sciences may dim through the grip of passion upon the disciples in certain states. Then, every soul knows what it has prepared.” The whole sūra, read at this level, becomes the picture of a present resurrection lived in the wayfarer's heart: he knows, in these crossings, eclipses, opacities, furnaces and gardens drawn near. And each crossing brings him back to this knowing: what I have prepared.

mots.def: aḥḍara: to bring forth, to prepare.

versets.trad: No!… I swear by the planets
versets.commentaire: al-khunnas (الخُنَّس): Qushayrī: “the stars when they hide.” According to certain readings, these are the five wandering planets — Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn — which “hide” by day. The word comes from khanasa, “to withdraw, to hide.” The sūra swears by these furtive stars.

mots.def: fa- + emphatic lā of the oath.
khunnas: plural; the ones that hide.

versets.trad: that orbit and disappear,
versets.commentaire: al-jawār al-kunnas: the runners and the den-keepers. Jārī says the fluid course; kunnas, the return to the lair (as the antelope returns to its couch). A splendid image of the planets in passage, that appear, orbit, and withdraw.

mots.def: kunnas: plural; those that return to their lair.

versets.trad: by the night when it falls,
versets.commentaire: ʿasʿas: Qushayrī: “it has come and advanced.” A rare and beautiful verb, doubled to mime the slow movement of the night's arrival.

mots.def: ʿasʿasa: to advance, to come, to withdraw (verb of double meaning).

versets.trad: by the dawn when it breathes its breath:
versets.commentaire: tanaffasa: it has breathed. Qushayrī: “it came out from the belly of the night.” A splendid image: dawn is the night taking its first breath, exhaling its first breath after the long holding-back.

versets.trad: this Word is truly that of a noble Envoy,
versets.commentaire: Response to the oaths: « This Quran is the word of a noble Envoy — that is, Gabriel, peace be upon him » (Qushayrī). It is Gabriel here as transmitter, not the Prophet Muḥammad who will be named a little later.

versets.ar: ذِي قُوَّةٍ عِندَ ذِي الْعَرْشِ مَكِينٍ
versets.tr: dhī quwwatin ʿinda dhī l-ʿarshi makīn
versets.trad: endowed with great strength and solid standing with the Master of the Throne,
versets.commentaire: makīn: Qushayrī: “firm in his position. His power is such that he uprooted the city of the people of Lot and overturned it.” Gabriel is not only messenger — he is also the instrument of divine justice when needed.

versets.trad: obeyed there and trustworthy.
versets.commentaire: muṭāʿ: obeyed (among the angels); amīn: trustworthy, faithful guardian. Four qualities of Gabriel enumerated: strength, position, obedience, fidelity.

mots.def: muṭāʿ: passive participle of aṭāʿa; obeyed.

versets.trad: Your companion is not possessed!
versets.commentaire: ṣāḥibukum: your companion — Muḥammad. The sūra turns the Meccans' own accusations against them. And God's defence of His messenger is sober, and formal.

mots.def: majnūn: possessed by a jinn, mad.

versets.trad: He saw him (the angel) at the luminous horizon,
versets.commentaire: raʾāhu bi-l-ufuq al-mubīn: he saw him at the clear horizon. Qushayrī: “Muḥammad saw Gabriel at the clear horizon the night of the Miʿrāj. And it is said: he saw his Lord, while he was at the clear horizon.” The double reading is precious: the vision may be of Gabriel — or, higher, of the Lord Himself during the nocturnal Ascent.

mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: and he does not jealously withhold the mystery.
versets.commentaire: ḍanīn (ضَنِين) — Qushayrī: “stingy.” According to some readings with ẓ instead of ḍ: “suspect.” The Prophet, in both readings, is exonerated: he does not withhold for himself what God reveals to him of the Unseen; nor is he suspect of having made it up. All that he knows, he says.

mots.def: ghayb: what is hidden, the Unseen.
ḍanīn: stingy, withholding; or (with ẓ) suspect.

versets.trad: This is not the word of an accursed demon!
versets.commentaire: Triple affirmation: neither is Muḥammad possessed (v. 22), nor does he withhold the Unseen (v. 24), nor does the Word come from a demon. Pure origin: Gabriel sent by God.

versets.trad: Where then are you going?
versets.commentaire: A dizzying question. Qushayrī prolongs it with particular Sufi force: “For how long will you wander in the valleys of conjectures and suppositions? And where are you going, turning away from the contemplation of the places of the Truth? Why not return to your Master in what has pleased you and in what has afflicted you?” Fa-ayna tadhhabūn is not a geographical question: it is the deepest interrogation one can address to a life. Where are you going — and what have you gone seeking so far from His house?

versets.trad: This is but a Reminder addressed to the worlds
versets.commentaire: The Quran is a dhikr — a reminder, a memorial. And it is addressed to the ʿālamīn, the worlds — that is, to every creature and every age. The universal Reminder.

versets.trad: for him among you who wills to follow the straight way.
versets.commentaire: yastaqīm: to stand straight, to follow the straight way. The root q-w-m says verticality, rectitude. Istiqāma is one of the master-words of Muslim spirituality: standing upright, without deviating, on the path of God. And Qushayrī, soberly, says: “what has been said on rectitude has been said.”

mots.def: istaqāma: to stand straight, to follow the straight way.

versets.ar: وَمَا تَشَاءُونَ إِلَّا أَن يَشَاءَ اللَّهُ رَبُّ الْعَالَمِينَ
versets.tr: wa-mā tashāʾūna illā an yashāʾa Llāhu rabbu l-ʿālamīn
versets.trad: But you will only will if God wills, He, the Lord of the worlds.
versets.commentaire: A fundamental verse on the relation between human will and divine will. You would want to stand straight (v. 28)? You can only because God, first, has willed it for you. Every human will derives from a prior divine will. Qushayrī, in a word: “that they will” — that is, their willing itself is subordinated to the supreme Willing. It is one of the Quranic foundations of tawakkul (trustful surrender): one surrenders not because one has renounced willing, but because one has understood that all willing is, at its origin, a gift.
